rules.name: Ability Score Improvement
rules.summary: A universal level-up reward, not a class feature — every class gets it at the same levels.

sections.heading: What it is
paragraphs: An Ability Score Improvement (ASI) lets you raise your ability scores as you level. It is the standard way a character's numbers climb over time: a higher primary ability means better attack and damage rolls, higher save DCs on the effects you force, and stronger saving throws of your own.
This is a base rule of the system. It applies identically to every class, so it lives here in the Rules rather than being printed as a feature on each class sheet.

sections.heading: When you gain one
paragraphs: Every class gains an Ability Score Improvement at 4th level, and again at 8th, 12th, 16th, and 19th level. (Levels beyond 5 are outside the current build scope but the cadence is fixed here so it never has to be re-decided per class.)

sections.heading: What you may do with it
list: Increase one ability score by 2, or
Increase two ability scores by 1 each.
No ability score can go above 20 this way.
If your table uses feats, you may take a feat instead of the score increase.

rules.name: Armor & AC
rules.summary: Armor sets your AC — the number an attack must beat before you even Parry.

sections.heading: AC comes first
paragraphs: Armor Class (AC) is the number an attacker must meet or beat on its attack roll to hit you. Only a hit can be Parried, so armor helps by keeping attacks from reaching the Parry step at all. AC and Parry are two layers of defense, not the same one.

sections.heading: Armor categories
list: Clothing (e.g. robes): AC 10 + your full Dexterity modifier. Needs no proficiency — the option for casters with no armor training. Same as being unarmored.
Light: base AC + your FULL Dexterity modifier. Quiet and nimble; favors high-Dex characters.
Medium: base AC + Dexterity modifier, but capped at +2. A high-Dex character gains no more than +2 from it.
Heavy: a FIXED base AC — no Dexterity added at all. The best flat AC, but Dexterity never helps; the strongest pieces also require a minimum Strength.
Shield: +2 AC on top of whatever armor (or clothing) you wear.

sections.heading: Armor does not affect Parry
paragraphs: Wearing heavy armor does not make you worse at Parrying. Some classes parry by bracing and absorbing rather than dodging, so armor is never a penalty to it. How good you are at Parrying is set by your class's Parry DC, not by what you wear.

sections.heading: Starter vs bought armor
paragraphs: Not every armor is available at character creation. Starter armor is the basic kit you can begin with: padded, leather, and studded leather; hide and scale; chain mail; and a shield.

Bought armor is a premium upgrade — breastplate, plate, and the like — that you acquire later by purchase or loot, not at creation. A fresh character does not start in plate. Actual prices are left to the DM and the campaign.

sections.heading: Strength requirements
paragraphs: Some heavy armors list a minimum Strength score (Chain Mail 13, Splint and Plate 15). You can still wear the armor without it, but your speed drops by 10 feet while you do. Light and medium armors never have a Strength requirement.

sections.heading: Why some armors look alike
paragraphs: Two armors can share a base AC and differ only in stealth or Strength needs — for example a breastplate and scale mail are both base 14, but the breastplate lets you sneak while scale mail does not. In standard D&D the other differentiator is COST, which is left to your DM here. Once prices are set, the cheaper-but-louder or cheaper-but-weaker option becomes a real budget choice; until then, pick by stealth, Strength, and Dexterity fit.

rules.name: Attacks & Damage
rules.summary: Your attacks are your weapons — how to hit and how damage is calculated.

sections.heading: Making an attack
paragraphs: To attack, you take the Attack action and strike with a weapon you are proficient with. Roll a d20 and add your ability modifier and your proficiency bonus; if the total meets or beats the target's Armor Class (AC), you hit.

There is no separate list of 'attacks' for a class — a class attacks with the weapons it is proficient with. Each class page lists those under Attacks, and every weapon's full stats are in the Weapons tab.

sections.heading: Which ability?
paragraphs: Melee weapons normally use Strength; finesse and ranged weapons use Dexterity. Some classes change this: the Puppeteer's Strings of Will lets his Control Wires use Charisma instead. A class feature always tells you when a different ability applies.

sections.heading: Damage on a hit
paragraphs: A hit deals the weapon's damage die plus the same ability modifier you used to attack. A Rapier (1d8) with a +3 modifier deals 1d8 + 3.

Class features can add more. The Puppeteer's Puppet's Spite adds an extra Charisma modifier against a strung enemy, so a Control Wires hit — normally 1d4 + Charisma via Strings of Will — becomes 1d4 + Charisma + Charisma on a strung foe.

sections.heading: Where to find damage
paragraphs: Every weapon's damage die, type, properties, and parry profile live on its page in the Weapons tab. Your class page's Attacks section shows the weapons that class actually uses.

rules.name: Parry & Dodging
rules.summary: Active defense — roll to dodge, graze, or overcommit when you are hit.

sections.heading: How Parry works
paragraphs: When an attack roll hits you (beats your AC) and you can see the attacker, you may spend your reaction to Parry. Roll a flat d20 — no modifier is added, because your bonuses are already baked into your Parry DC — and compare the result to your effective Parry DC.

sections.heading: The three outcomes
list: Roll ABOVE your Parry DC: Full Dodge — you take no damage at all.
Roll EXACTLY your Parry DC: Grazing Parry — you take half damage.
Roll BELOW your Parry DC: Overcommitted — you take +50% damage (you committed to the dodge and got caught).

sections.heading: Your Parry DC (lower is better)
paragraphs: Each class has its own Parry Base DC, shown on its class page. Your effective DC for a given attack = class base DC + your weapon's parry modifier + situational modifiers, minus your proficiency bonus (only if you are proficient with the weapon in hand) minus half your defense-ability modifier, with a floor of 3.
So a weapon well-suited to defense, a higher character level, and a good defense ability all push the DC lower, meaning you Full Dodge more often. A ranged or improvised defense pushes it higher.

sections.heading: Cost and timing
paragraphs: Parry costs your reaction, and you have only one reaction per round. A round you spend dodging is a round you cannot make an opportunity attack, and you can fully dodge only one attack per round.

You may Parry any attack you can see that hits you — including an opportunity attack, even one you provoke on your own turn by moving. You cannot Parry while stunned, incapacitated, paralyzed, unconscious, unable to see the attacker, or with no reaction left.

sections.heading: Riposte
paragraphs: On a Full Dodge, some classes can strike back — a riposte. Each class's page describes its own riposte, if it has one.

rules.name: Reaction & Opportunity Attacks
rules.summary: One reaction per round — Parry OR opportunity attack, never both.

sections.heading: One reaction
paragraphs: Every creature — you and monsters alike — has exactly one reaction per round, which refreshes at the start of its own turn. Parrying, making an opportunity attack, and any other reaction all draw from that single reaction.

sections.heading: The tank-or-parry choice
paragraphs: Because you only get one reaction, being hit forces a choice: spend it to Parry now, or take the hit and save your reaction for a bigger threat later in the round.

If several enemies get to attack you in a round — for example, when flashy movement provokes multiple opportunity attacks — you can only Parry one of them. The rest land in full. That exposure is the price of moving boldly around the battlefield.

rules.name: Weapon Mastery
rules.summary: Every weapon carries a default combat maneuver any proficient wielder can use — your baseline 'topple / cleave / vex' options, no class feature required.

sections.heading: What it is
paragraphs: Beyond a class's own features, each weapon has a Mastery property: a small maneuver that triggers on your ordinary weapon attacks. Any character proficient with the weapon can use its Mastery — it is the shared combat vocabulary every class has, on top of its engine and features.

A Mastery triggers as part of the attack that sets it off (on a hit, or a miss for Graze), costs no action or resource, and — unless the Mastery says otherwise — applies each time you hit. Where a Mastery forces a save, the DC is 8 + your proficiency bonus + the ability modifier you attacked with.

sections.heading: The masteries
list: Cleave — On a melee hit, make one attack against a second creature within 5 feet of the first and within your reach; on that hit deal the weapon's damage but not your ability modifier. Once per turn.
Graze — On a miss, still deal damage equal to the ability modifier you attacked with (the weapon's damage type).
Nick — When you make the extra attack granted by the Light property, you can make it as part of your Attack action instead of as a bonus action (once per turn).
Push — On a hit, if the target is Large or smaller you can push it up to 10 feet straight away from you.
Sap — On a hit, the target has disadvantage on its next attack roll before the start of your next turn.
Slow — On a hit that deals damage, reduce the target's speed by 10 feet until the start of your next turn.
Topple — On a hit, the target must succeed on a Constitution saving throw (DC = 8 + your proficiency bonus + your attacking ability modifier) or be knocked prone.
Vex — On a hit that deals damage, you have advantage on your next attack roll against that same target before the end of your next turn.

sections.heading: Where to find it
paragraphs: Each weapon lists its Mastery on its own page and in the Attacks table on every class sheet, so you can see at a glance what maneuver your weapon grants.

rules.name: Weapon Properties
rules.summary: What the tags on a weapon (Finesse, Light, Reach, Thrown, Versatile...) actually mean.

sections.heading: Reading a weapon
paragraphs: Every weapon lists a few properties that change how you attack with it. They appear on the weapon's page and in the Attacks table on each class sheet — hover any property name there to see its meaning.

sections.heading: The properties
list: Finesse — Use Strength or Dexterity (your choice) for your attack and damage rolls with it. (A class feature may further change which ability you use — e.g. the Joker uses Charisma.)
Light — Small and easy to handle. Holding a Light weapon in each hand lets you use a bonus action to make one extra attack with the second weapon (two-weapon fighting).
Heavy — Large and unwieldy; Small creatures have disadvantage on attack rolls made with it.
Reach — Adds 5 feet to your reach when you attack with it, and for opportunity attacks.
Thrown — You can throw it to make a ranged attack, using the same ability modifier you would for a melee attack with it.
Two-handed — You need both hands to attack with it.
Versatile — You can use it one- or two-handed; used two-handed it deals the larger 'versatile' damage die shown on its page.
Ammunition — It needs ammunition to make a ranged attack; you draw a piece as part of the attack.
Loading — You can fire only one piece of ammunition when you attack with it, regardless of how many attacks you could normally make.
Special — It has unusual rules described in the weapon's own text.

classes.name: Acrobat
classes.flavor: An aerial rogue who turns falling, swinging, and sprinting into a weapon, never quite where the last swing landed.

proficiencies.skills: Choose two from Acrobatics, Athletics, Performance, Persuasion, Sleight of Hand, and Stealth

classes.parryReskin: You never block the hit at all — a twist of the spine, a mid-air redirect, a heel that was never fully planted turns solid contact into a near-miss.
classes.riposte: On a Full Dodge (your Parry d20 roll comes up strictly above your effective Parry DC), you may spend 1 Momentum to lash back at the triggering attacker for damage of your weapon's type (the lash-back hits automatically, no attack roll). The lash-back die grows as you level, on its own curve: 1d4 at 1st level, 1d6 at 3rd, 1d8 at 5th, 1d10 at 7th, 1d12 at 9th, and 1d20 at 12th. This is a small reward for a clean dodge, not a full attack.

engine.name: Momentum
engine.description: A personal pool of kinetic flow built from constant motion — sprints, spins, swings, and falls turned into finesse. The Acrobat's sole resource.
engine.generation: You build Momentum through motion, and only through motion — no technique that spends Momentum ever also grants it. Gain 1 Momentum (at most once per turn from this movement source) when you move at least 10 feet toward, away from, or around a hostile creature on your turn, or when you take the Dash or Disengage action. You also gain 1 Momentum the first time each turn you use Wall-Run. Trapeze Swing, Strike-and-Vanish, Momentum Strike, Flow State, and Riposte only spend Momentum — they never generate it.
engine.spend: Spend Momentum in 1-point increments, at no action cost beyond the triggering action, to fuel your features (bonus damage, forced movement, extra mobility, or momentum-gated attacks). Unspent Momentum carries over between rounds but drains to 0 if you finish a short or long rest without spending it.

features.name: Momentum
features.description: You gain the Momentum pool, a resource built by moving with speed and spent to fuel your acrobatics and strikes. See your Momentum engine for how it is generated, spent, and capped.

features.name: Counterflow
features.description: Turning a blow aside leaves the attacker overextended, and you flow straight into the opening. When you successfully Parry an attack — a Full Dodge or a Grazing Parry, meaning your Parry d20 comes up at least equal to your effective Parry DC — the next attack roll you make before the end of your next turn has advantage. An Overcommitted Parry (a roll below your DC) grants nothing. Because you have only one reaction, this triggers at most once per round.

features.name: Wall-Run
features.description: As part of your movement, once per turn, you can run up to half your speed along a vertical surface or across something that couldn't normally support your weight (a wall, a taut rope, a row of chairs), provided you end that move on solid ground or something you can grab. This is a signature move: it generates Momentum.

features.name: Trapeze Swing
features.description: As a bonus action and a signature move, you conjure your own rigging from nowhere — a bar, rope, or ring that appears exactly where you need it — and swing up to 30 feet in any direction, ignoring difficult terrain, for 5 feet of movement and 1 Momentum. Your aerial style is flashy, not safe: leaving a creature's reach during the swing provokes opportunity attacks as normal. At the end of the swing you can make one melee weapon attack against a creature within 5 feet. As a flourish, you may spend 3 additional Momentum to make that attack against every hostile creature you pass within 5 feet during the swing instead of just one, each targeted once. You can use Trapeze Swing at most once per turn. (See Flow State at 5th level: on a turn where you use Trapeze Swing, you cannot also trigger Flow State's extra attack.)

features.name: Momentum Strike
features.description: When you hit a Large or smaller creature with a melee weapon attack, you can spend 1 Momentum to deal an extra [[1d6]] damage and force it to make a Strength saving throw against your {{save DC|8 + proficiency bonus + Dexterity modifier}}. On a failed save, you shove it 5 feet or knock it prone (your choice).

features.name: Strike-and-Vanish
features.description: After you hit a creature with a melee weapon attack, you can spend 2 Momentum to move up to half your speed without provoking opportunity attacks and attempt to Hide (per the normal Hide rules — you become hidden only if you end with total cover or out of the target's line of sight). As a flourish, you may spend 2 additional Momentum (4 total) to move your full speed instead of half — a clean disengage out of every enemy's reach (still no invisibility). You can use Strike-and-Vanish once per turn at 3rd level, twice per turn at 5th, three times at 9th, and four times at 12th. This is a signature move.

meta.uses: 1 / turn (2 at L5, 3 at L9, 4 at L12)

features.name: Flowing Reflexes
features.description: A clean parry doesn't end your turn's rhythm — it feeds it. Once per turn, when you successfully Parry an attack (a Full Dodge or a Grazing Parry), you immediately regain your reaction. This regained reaction cannot be spent to Parry again — use it for an opportunity attack or another reaction feature. If your Parry d20 came up a natural 20, you regain two reactions this way instead of one (still none able to Parry again). Any reaction regained this way lasts only until the start of your next turn; if you have not spent it by then, it is lost. Because a Parry still stops only one attack per round, this never makes you a surer dodger — it turns a clean deflection into extra tempo.

features.name: Momentum Reserve
features.description: Your Momentum cap increases to 6, and moving through a space occupied by an ally no longer costs extra movement.

features.name: Aerial Evasion
features.description: You are the one class whose dodging reaches beyond attack rolls. When you are subjected to an effect that allows you to make a Dexterity saving throw for half damage (a fireball, a trap, a breath weapon, or similar area effect), you instead take no damage on a success and only half damage on a failure. This is the sole exception to the rule that saving-throw and area effects can't be Parried.

features.name: Flow State
features.description: Once on each of your turns, if you have 3 or more Momentum and have not already gained an attack this turn from Trapeze Swing, you can spend 3 Momentum to make one additional weapon attack as part of the same action you used to make a melee weapon attack. Flow State's extra attack and Trapeze Swing's attack are mutually exclusive within a single turn — at most one of the two can add an attack on top of your normal action-attack, so no single creature can be struck by more than two of your attacks in a turn (your action attack plus one). Trapeze Swing's flourish can still spread additional single hits across several different creatures you pass within 5 feet — that is crowd damage, not focused damage, and it costs Momentum and provokes an opportunity attack from every creature you leave.

keyStats.label: Maneuver Save DC
keyStats.note: Save DC for effects your features force (e.g. Momentum Strike's shove or knock prone).

keyStats.label: Momentum Cap
keyStats.note: Most Momentum you can hold at once.

keyStats.label: Parry Base DC
keyStats.note: Best parrier in the roster; lower is better.

classes.name: Doppelganger
classes.flavor: A duplicator and trickster who is never quite where the last blow landed, fighting alongside a handful of semi-real reflections of himself.

proficiencies.skills: Choose two from Acrobatics, Deception, Insight, Perception, Sleight of Hand, and Stealth

classes.parryReskin: A Clone lurches into the blow's path at the last possible instant and takes the hit for you — you were never quite where the blade landed, only where you left an afterimage standing. This is pure narration: it does not change the Parry math already resolved (a Grazing Parry or Overcommitted result still deals whatever damage it deals). For a Clone that absorbs a hit entirely, see Mirror Image.
classes.riposte: On a Full Dodge (your Parry d20 roll comes up strictly above your effective Parry DC), you may create one Clone (see your Clone engine) in an unoccupied space within 5 feet of you, at no action cost and without spending your bonus action. This follows your normal Clone cap — if you are already at your cap, you must let an existing Clone drop first. A small, thematic reward for turning aside a hit clean, not a counterattack.

engine.name: Clones
engine.description: Semi-real mirror-duplicates of yourself, each occupying its own space, wearing your face and frozen in the pose you struck the moment it was made. A Clone is not a combat pet — it never takes actions of its own — it's a body double for a blade, an eye, or your own attacks to work through. A Clone is a snapshot: it copies every benefit affecting you at the instant it is created (advantage, damage riders, a temporary weapon enchantment, and the like) and keeps them for its whole life even if yours end, but it never gains anything applied to you afterward; its AC equals yours and it has no hit points. Clones do not move on their own once placed — you reshape the picture by spawning new Clones where you want them or by using Swap. Because a Clone is a sliver of your own living body rather than an illusion, it is your Constitution — your raw vitality — that powers your duplicates: your Constitution modifier sets their save DCs and is added to any damage a Clone deals — a Mirror Barrage swing, a Mirror Assault strike, a Decoy Detonation burst — in place of the Dexterity your own strikes use, even as your own blades still strike with a duelist’s Dexterity.
engine.generation: Create one Clone, as a bonus action, in an unoccupied space you can see within 30 feet of you; it appears already mimicking your exact appearance and current pose. You also gain a Clone at no action cost via Riposte, and via any other feature that says so. You may never have more Clones active at once than your Clone cap; creating a new one beyond your cap requires an existing Clone to drop first.
engine.spend: Clones aren't spent from one fixed pool all at once — specific features use them in specific ways. Swap teleports you into a Clone's space without destroying it. Mirror Barrage has every Clone in reach make one half-damage attack, without destroying them. Mirror Image destroys a Clone within 5 feet to fully absorb a hit that would otherwise land on you. Decoy Detonation destroys a Clone to burst it for damage and knockback. Mirror Assault (5th level) consumes a Clone to deliver an extra attack from its space. A Clone also drops on its own, destroying no other resource, the moment it ends up more than 30 feet from you, the instant you become incapacitated (all your Clones drop at once), or the first time any attack roll succeeds against it — it shares your AC but has no hit points of its own, so one confirmed hit and it's gone.
engine.cap: 2 (L1–4) · 3 (L5–8) · 4 (L9+) — Hall of Mirrors adds +1 from L5

features.name: Clones
features.description: You gain the Clones engine. As a bonus action, you create one Clone — a semi-real mirror-duplicate of yourself, mimicking your appearance and pose — in an unoccupied space you can see within 30 feet (45 at 5th level). At the instant it appears, a Clone copies your exact state: every benefit currently affecting you (advantage, damage riders, a temporary weapon enchantment, and so on) carries onto the Clone and stays for as long as it exists, even after your own version ends; it does not gain anything applied to you later. You can have up to your Clone cap (your proficiency bonus) active at once; creating one beyond the cap drops an existing Clone. A Clone shares your AC but has no hit points — a single hit destroys it — does not move on its own once placed, and drops if it ends more than 30 feet from you or you become incapacitated. Your other features reposition and spend Clones; see your Clone engine for the full detail.

features.name: Mirror Barrage
features.description: You act, and every reflection acts with you. As a bonus action, each of your Clones that has a creature within its reach makes one weapon attack from its own space against a target it can reach, using your attack roll and whatever weapon and benefits it copied when it was created. On a hit, a Clone deals half its damage, rounded down — and because a Clone strikes with your vitality rather than your aim, it adds your Constitution modifier where your own attack would add Dexterity: roll the weapon's damage die, add your Constitution modifier and any riders the Clone copied, then halve the total. Several Clones positioned around the same target all strike it; Clones spread across different enemies each strike their own. The Clones are not destroyed by attacking — they simply swing. You can use Mirror Barrage once per turn.

features.name: Mirror Image
features.description: Your Clones double as decoys that can take a hit meant for you. Whenever a creature you can see hits you with an attack, then — before you decide whether to Parry — roll a d6 to see if a decoy takes the blow instead. The redirect chance rises with how many Clones you have out: one Clone redirects on a 1 (1-in-6), two Clones on a 1-2 (2-in-6), three Clones on a 1-3 (3-in-6, the maximum). If it redirects: the attack strikes a Clone within 5 feet of you, that Clone is destroyed, you take no damage, and your reaction stays free (this doesn't use it). If it doesn't redirect: the hit lands as normal and you may still spend your reaction to Parry it. Mirror Image sits in front of your Parry as a bonus chance to avoid a hit entirely — a supplement, never a replacement.

features.name: Swap
features.description: As a bonus action, you may teleport-swap places with one of your Clones within 30 feet of you: you instantly appear in the Clone's space and it instantly appears in yours, each facing however you choose. Neither of you provokes opportunity attacks from this movement, and the Clone itself is not destroyed — it simply continues existing wherever it ends up, still bound by its normal leash and still able to be hit, redirected onto, or spent like any other Clone. You can use Swap once per turn.

features.name: Decoy Detonation
features.description: As an action, you cause one of your Clones to burst in a flash of shattering reflections, destroying it. Because you choose which Clone, you decide where the blast lands — spawn a Clone into a cluster of enemies, or Swap one there, then detonate it. Each creature within 5 feet of that Clone must make a Dexterity saving throw against your {{save DC|8 + proficiency bonus + Constitution modifier}}. On a failed save a creature takes [[1d6+Con]] psychic damage and is shoved 5 feet straight away from the Clone's space; on a success it takes half as much damage and is not moved. From 3rd level, Splintered Reflexes also lets you trigger Decoy Detonation as a reaction on another creature's turn. You can trigger Decoy Detonation at most once per turn.

features.name: Flanking Reflection
features.description: When you make a melee weapon attack against a creature that has one of your Clones within 5 feet of it, you have advantage on the attack roll — the creature can't watch both you and your reflection at once. This costs nothing and doesn't destroy the Clone, but requires one to already be positioned correctly; it applies at most once per attack and doesn't stack with other sources of advantage on the same roll.

features.name: Splintered Reflexes
features.description: Your reflections let you answer more than one threat at once. You gain one additional reaction each round, for two total, both refreshing at the start of your turn. This extra reaction can be used only to make an opportunity attack or to activate a Clone feature that costs a reaction — most usefully, it lets you trigger Decoy Detonation on another creature's turn. It can never be used to Parry a second time: you still redirect only one hit per round onto a Clone, so the Acrobat remains the surer dodger. Each reaction is spent once; you choose which threat each one answers.

features.name: Mirror Assault
features.description: This is your 5th-level damage step — the Doppelganger's version of Extra Attack, but earned rather than free. Once per turn, when you take the Attack action, if one of your Clones is within 5 feet of your target, you can make one extra weapon attack that strikes from that Clone's space, using your normal attack roll; its damage is the weapon's die plus your Constitution modifier (a Clone's blow draws on your vitality, not your Dexterity). That Clone is then destroyed — it spends itself delivering the blow. Unlike a plain Extra Attack this one is conditional: you need a Clone already positioned next to the target, and it costs you that Clone.

features.name: Hall of Mirrors
features.description: Your reflections run deeper and reach farther: your Clone cap increases by 1 (in addition to whatever your proficiency bonus already grants it), and the range on Clone creation, Swap, and a Clone's leash all increase from 30 to 45 feet.

keyStats.label: Save DC
keyStats.note: Save DC for effects your features force (e.g. Decoy Detonation).

keyStats.label: Clones Cap
keyStats.note: Most active Clone tokens you can have.

keyStats.label: Parry Base DC
keyStats.note: Lower is better.

keyStats.label: Reactions
keyStats.note: Second reaction: opportunity attack or clone reaction only, never a 2nd Parry.

classes.name: Joker
classes.flavor: A chaos menace with a deck of razor cards and a grin that never quite fits the moment — he wins by making sure no one, least of all himself, knows what happens next.

proficiencies.skills: Choose two from Acrobatics, Deception, Intimidation, Performance, Persuasion, and Sleight of Hand

classes.parryReskin: A lunatic, last-second contortion — a mad twist of the spine or a limb bent the wrong way at the worst possible instant. He never dodges the sane way, and the crowd can never tell whether it was skill or dumb luck.
classes.riposte: On a Full Dodge (your Parry d20 roll comes up strictly above your effective Parry DC), the triggering attacker must succeed on a Charisma saving throw against your gambit DC or suffer a minor mishap: its next attack roll before the start of your next turn is made with disadvantage.

engine.name: Mayhem
engine.description: A small pool of chaotic energy built from doing risky, unpredictable things in a fight and spent to draw Wild Cards and fuel Gambits. The Joker's sole resource — deliberately kept small so its swings stay bounded rather than build into a snowball.
engine.generation: Gain 1 Mayhem the first time each turn you deal damage to a creature, AND gain 1 Mayhem the first time each turn you take damage, AND gain 1 Mayhem the first time each turn you roll a natural 1 or a natural 20 on an attack roll, ability check, or saving throw — three separate sources, each capped at once per turn (all three triggering in the same turn grants 3 Mayhem total; a source that already triggered this turn grants no more). This works from 1st level.
engine.spend: Spend Mayhem in 1-point increments, at no action cost beyond the triggering feature, to draw Wild Cards, fuel your Gambits, and pay for your other Mayhem-keyed features. Unspent Mayhem carries over between rounds but drains to 0 if you finish a short or long rest without spending it.

features.name: Mayhem
features.description: You gain the Mayhem pool, a resource built from doing risky, chaotic things in a fight and spent to fuel your Wild Cards and Gambits. See your Mayhem engine for exactly how it is generated, spent, and capped.

features.name: Wild Talent
features.description: Your chaos answers your personality, not your hands: you use your Charisma modifier, instead of Strength or Dexterity, for attack and damage rolls you make with a finesse weapon you're proficient with (your Razor Cards, Harlequin's Cane, or Dagger). Any saving throw a Joker feature or Wild Card forces on a creature uses your {{gambit DC|8 + proficiency bonus + Charisma modifier}}.

features.name: Wild Card
features.description: As a bonus action, spend 1 Mayhem to draw a Wild Card: roll a d8 and immediately resolve the matching result below, with no further action needed. Any saving throw listed is a Charisma saving throw against your gambit DC. Drawing costs a bonus action and 1 Mayhem, so it happens at most once per turn.

options.name: Ace Up the Sleeve
options.effect: You have advantage on the next attack roll or saving throw you make before the end of your next turn.

options.name: Marked Card
options.effect: The next attack you hit with before the end of your next turn deals an extra [[1d6]] damage of that weapon's damage type.

options.name: Contagious Cackle
options.effect: Choose one creature you can see within 30 feet; it must succeed on a saving throw or its next attack roll before the start of your next turn is made with disadvantage.

options.name: Quick Vanish
options.effect: Until the end of your turn your speed increases by 10 feet, and you don't provoke opportunity attacks when you move away from a hostile creature this turn.

options.name: Second Wind
options.effect: You regain [[1d4]] + your Charisma modifier hit points (minimum 1).

options.name: Wild Swap
options.effect: The deck turns on you for a moment — choose one hostile creature within 30 feet of you; it has advantage on the next attack roll it makes against you before the start of your next turn.

options.name: Static Shock
options.effect: A spark of raw chaos jumps back through your own hand and you take [[1d4]] psychic damage.

options.name: Encore
options.effect: If you have at least 1 Mayhem remaining, you may immediately spend it to draw again, rerolling on this table and resolving only the new result (this draw itself does nothing); if you have no Mayhem left, this draw has no further effect.

meta.save: Cha save vs Gambit DC (some results)

features.name: Chaotic Parry
features.description: Once per turn when you Parry, after seeing the result, you may spend 1 Mayhem to reroll your Parry d20 — you must use the new roll, even if it is worse. Every Parry of yours narrates as a lunatic, last-second contortion rather than a clean block; the crowd can never tell whether it was skill or dumb luck.

features.name: Trickster's Luck
features.description: The deck looks after its own. A number of times equal to your {{Charisma modifier (min 1)|set from your stats on your character sheet}}, which you regain when you finish a long rest, when you roll below your effective Parry DC (an Overcommitted result), you can treat that Parry as a Grazing Parry instead — taking half damage rather than +50%. You decide after seeing the roll. This doesn't change your Parry DC, only how badly a failed one lands.

features.name: Gambit
features.description: As a bonus action, spend 1 Mayhem to attempt a Gambit — a small, rule-bending trick. Choose one:

options.name: Switcheroo
options.effect: Choose one creature you can see within 30 feet of you; you and it instantly swap positions, each appearing in the other's space, and neither of you provokes opportunity attacks from the move (each of you must be able to occupy the other's space). Close on a target, drag a dangerous foe off a fragile ally and take its place yourself, or trade spots with an ally to slip out of a bad one. A willing creature swaps automatically; an unwilling creature can refuse by succeeding on a Charisma saving throw against your gambit DC (on a success, neither of you moves).

options.name: Jinx
options.effect: Choose one creature you can see within 30 feet of you and stack the deck against it. The first attack roll or saving throw it makes before the start of your next turn, it must roll twice and use the lower result. Unlike your other tricks, this also spoils a saving throw — set it up on your turn before an ally's spell or your own gambit lands. A hostile creature can shrug the jinx off by succeeding on a Charisma saving throw against your gambit DC when you declare it (on a success, the jinx has no effect).

options.name: Fortune's Favor
options.effect: Choose one ally within 30 feet of you; the next attack roll or saving throw it makes before the start of your next turn is made with advantage.

features.name: Loaded Deck
features.description: The first time each turn you hit a creature with a finesse weapon you're proficient with, you can spend 1 Mayhem to deal an extra [[1d6]] damage of that weapon's damage type. If the attack roll that hit was a natural 20, you deal this extra damage without spending Mayhem, and its dice are doubled along with the attack's other damage dice, as normal for a critical hit.

features.name: Contagious Panic
features.description: As an action, spend 2 Mayhem to unravel the nerve of up to two creatures you can see within 30 feet of you and within 10 feet of each other. Each must succeed on a Charisma saving throw against your gambit DC or have disadvantage on the next attack roll it makes before the start of your next turn. This deals no damage and imposes no condition beyond that single disadvantage — a moment of dread, not a stun.

features.name: Full House
features.description: When you spend Mayhem to draw a Wild Card, you may spend 1 additional Mayhem (2 total) to draw two cards instead of one. If you do, resolve only one of the two results — your choice — and discard the other; you never resolve both at once.

features.name: Ace in the Hole
features.description: Once per turn, when you hit a creature with an attack using a finesse weapon you're proficient with, you can spend 2 Mayhem to treat the attack as a critical hit, doubling its damage dice as normal for a critical hit. This doesn't stack with an attack that was already a natural-20 critical hit — Ace in the Hole turns an ordinary hit into a showstopper, it doesn't make an already-crit hit bigger. Forcing the deck this hard leaves you exposed: until the start of your next turn, attack rolls against you have advantage.

keyStats.label: Gambit DC
keyStats.note: Save DC for Wild Cards, Gambits, and every other Joker effect that forces a save.

keyStats.label: Mayhem Cap
keyStats.note: Most Mayhem you can hold at once.

keyStats.label: Parry Base DC
keyStats.note: Dead centre of the roster; lower is better.

classes.name: Juggler
classes.flavor: The circus juggler as duelist — a constant cascade of knives, weighted orbs, and clubs, every one a weapon he can loose or keep. He fights in bursts: hurl a storm of thrown steel and stand exposed, or hold a few back to knock the answer aside.

proficiencies.skills: Choose two from Acrobatics, Performance, Sleight of Hand, Insight, and Deception

classes.parryReskin: You don't dodge — you deflect, knocking an incoming blow aside with an implement still in your hand. The more of your set you have already hurled, the less you have left to guard with; empty-handed, all you can do is wait to catch them back at the start of your next turn.
classes.riposte: On a Full Dodge (your Parry d20 comes up strictly above your effective Parry DC), you catch the blow on a held implement and flick it back in the same motion: the triggering attacker takes [[1d4]] damage of that implement's type, no attack roll and no cost beyond the Parry. You keep the implement — this is a deflection, not a throw.

engine.name: Juggle Set
engine.description: You are always juggling a set of light implements — throwing knives, weighted orbs, clubs. They are your ammunition and your guard at once. You never buy, gather, or track them: at the start of each of your turns you catch your whole set back to full. On your action you decide how many to throw; whatever you keep in hand is what you can Deflect with. This baseline is shared by every Juggler from 1st level; your subclass (chosen at 3rd level) shapes what your implements do.
engine.generation: At the start of each of your turns your set refills to full — you catch every implement back into the cascade, thrown or not. There is no other way to gain or lose implements; they are never spent for good.
engine.spend: On your Attack action you throw any number of implements, from 0 up to your Set Size, as your Barrage. Each implement thrown leaves your hand until your set refills, and every one you have thrown worsens your Deflect (with none in hand you cannot Parry at all).
engine.capFormula: proficiency bonus + 1 (+2 more from Grand Cascade at L5)

features.name: Juggle Set
features.description: You gain the Juggle Set — the ring of implements you always keep in the air. See your engine for exactly how it refills, how many you hold (your Set Size), and how throwing them feeds your Barrage and drains your Deflect. Your Set Size is 3 at 1st level and grows as you gain levels.

features.name: Barrage
features.description: When you take the Attack action, you loose your Barrage instead of making a normal attack: choose any number of implements from 0 up to your Set Size and throw them at targets you can see within normal thrown range (20 feet, or up to 60 at disadvantage). Each thrown implement is a separate ranged {{attack roll|d20 + Dexterity modifier + proficiency bonus}}. On a hit, the FIRST of your implements to land each turn deals [[1d4+Dex]]; every implement that hits after that deals only its [[1d4]] die, no ability modifier — a barrage is a flurry, not a dozen full-power strikes. Any Weapon Mastery your implements carry (such as Topple or Slow) triggers at most once per Barrage, however many implements hit. You may split them across different targets or pile them all onto one. Throwing 0 is a real choice — you make no attack but keep your full set in hand for defense (see Deflect).

features.name: Flourish
features.description: The more of your set you commit to a Barrage, the flashier the trick — but you get only ONE flourish per turn, decided by how many implements you throw. You gain the single benefit for your throw count, not the lower ones (throw 2 and you get the second effect only, not the first):

options.name: Throw 1
options.effect: Pinpoint — you have advantage on that attack roll. A single aimed throw made with your guard still up (you keep almost your whole set for Deflect).

options.name: Throw 2
options.effect: Off-Balance — a creature you hit with an implement this turn has disadvantage on its next attack roll before the start of your next turn.

options.name: Throw 3 or more
options.effect: Showstopper — for the rest of this turn your thrown implements score a critical hit on a roll of 19 or 20, the all-in flurry finding every gap. Committing three or more implements badly weakens your Deflect this round (and empties your hands entirely at low levels), so the crit-fishing costs you your guard.

features.name: Deflect
features.description: Your Parry is a deflection made with an implement still in hand, so how well you can do it depends on how many you are still holding. Your effective Parry DC is your base of 12 while you hold your full set, and it worsens by 1 for each implement you have thrown and not yet caught back — that is, +1 for each one short of a full set. If you are holding no implements at all, you cannot Parry until your set refills at the start of your next turn. Because the set refills every turn, this resets each round: a turn you throw everything is a turn you stand defenseless, and a turn you hold some back keeps your guard up at a worse DC. Deflecting still costs your reaction like any Parry.

features.name: Nimble Cascade
features.description: A juggler never plants his feet. As a bonus action on your turn, you can move up to half your speed, and that movement doesn't provoke opportunity attacks. Open the range before you empty your set, or slip away on a turn you're left defenseless.

features.name: Ricochet
features.description: Your implements carom off their targets. Once on each of your turns, when a thrown implement hits a creature during your Barrage, you can have it ricochet outward from that target: make a separate ranged attack roll against every other creature you can see within 15 feet of it, and on each hit the implement deals [[1d4+Dex]] damage (rolled separately for each). Only ONE implement ricochets per turn, no matter how many you throw — so a full 3-implement Barrage still gets a single ricochet. The ricochet is a free bonus of an implement you already threw: it doesn't cost you an implement or further worsen your Deflect.

features.name: Trick Shot
features.description: You don't just throw hard — you throw smart, reading angles and gaps mid-cascade. Once on each of your turns you can designate ONE implement in your Barrage as a Trick Shot — never more than that single implement, no matter how many you throw. On a hit, in addition to its normal damage, the target must succeed on a saving throw against your {{Trick Shot DC|6 + proficiency bonus + Intelligence modifier}} or suffer one effect of your choice (the save ability depends on the effect):

options.name: Blind
options.effect: An orb bursts across its eyes — Constitution save or blinded until the end of its next turn.

options.name: Trip
options.effect: A club takes its legs — Strength save or knocked prone.

options.name: Disarm
options.effect: A knife to the knuckles — Strength save or it drops one item it is holding (your choice), landing at its feet.

options.name: Pin
options.effect: The implement staples a limb in place — Dexterity save or its speed becomes 0 until the end of its next turn.

features.name: Grand Cascade
features.description: Your cascade widens. Your Set Size increases by 2 — it is now your proficiency bonus + 3 — so you juggle, hold, and can throw that many more implements each turn. Every extra one you keep still shores up your Deflect, and every extra one you throw still feeds your Barrage, Ricochet, and Trick Shot.

features.name: Chain Reaction
features.description: Keep the show going. Once per combat, you can choose to make your Ricochet chain: after it sprays its targets, pick one creature it hit and the implement caroms off and ricochets a second time from that creature's space — a separate ranged attack against every other creature you can see within 15 feet of it, dealing [[1d4+Dex]] on each hit, exactly like Ricochet. You decide when to trigger it; it does not require dropping a target.

keyStats.label: Set Size
keyStats.formula: proficiency bonus + 1 (Grand Cascade adds +2 more from L5)
keyStats.note: How many implements you hold and can throw each turn.

keyStats.label: Implement Damage
keyStats.note: First implement to hit each turn adds Dex; the rest deal the die only. Die scales at L5/11/17.

keyStats.label: Trick Shot DC
keyStats.note: Save DC for Trick Shot effects and your subclass's forced saves (from L3). Modest in practice since Int is a secondary stat.

keyStats.label: Parry Base DC
keyStats.note: At full hands; +1 per implement thrown, none held = no Parry. Lower is better.

classes.name: Puppeteer
classes.flavor: A marionettist who never means to enter the fight himself — a web of near-invisible wire turns foes into puppets and allies into partners, moved exactly where the show demands.

proficiencies.skills: Choose two from Deception, Insight, Intimidation, Performance, Persuasion, and Sleight of Hand

classes.parryReskin: You are no blocker at all — a curtain of near-invisible wire snaps up between you and the blow at the last instant, trying to deflect what your own body never could. It is a last resort, not a specialty: your effective Parry DC runs the worst in the roster, so a clean Full Dodge is rare, and more often the wire only barely turns aside what it can't fully stop.
classes.riposte: On a Full Dodge (your Parry d20 roll comes up strictly above your effective Parry DC), you may attach a String to the triggering attacker at no action cost and without spending your bonus action. This follows your normal String cap — if you are already at your cap, you must detach an existing String to make room. A small, thematic reward for a clean dodge, not a counterattack.

engine.name: Strings
engine.description: Lengths of near-invisible wire you fix to creatures around you. A String is not spent like currency — it is an attachment that stays in place, keying your control features to whatever it's tied to, until you choose to let go of it or something forces you to.
engine.generation: Attach a String to a creature you can see within 60 feet as a bonus action, or automatically at no action cost when you hit a creature with your Control Wires. You may never have more Strings attached at once than your String cap; attaching a new one beyond your cap requires detaching an existing String first (no action).
engine.spend: Strings aren't consumed to use your control features — they are the prerequisite for them. Manipulate, Tangle, Disrupting Pull, Guiding Pull, and Grand Marionette all require a String already attached to the target; using them doesn't remove the String. A String stays attached until you detach it (no action), the creature moves beyond your 60-foot leash range, or you become incapacitated, in which case all of your Strings snap at once.
engine.cap: 2 (L1–4) · 3 (L5–8) · 4 (L9+) — Web of Strings adds +1 from L5

features.name: Strings
features.description: You gain the Strings engine. As a bonus action, you attach a String to a creature you can see within 60 feet (90 at 5th level) — or attach one for free when you hit a creature with your Control Wires. You can maintain up to your String cap (your proficiency bonus) at once. Every other control feature you have keys off a creature you already have a String on; see your Strings engine for how Strings are maintained and lost.

features.name: Strings of Will
features.description: Your art runs on focus and anticipation, not muscle or bluster: you use your Wisdom modifier for attack and damage rolls made with a finesse weapon you're proficient with (your Control Wires, Dagger, or Whip). Any saving throw a Puppeteer feature forces on a creature uses your {{control DC|8 + proficiency bonus + Wisdom modifier}}.

features.name: Bodyguard Strings
features.description: You never meant to take a hit yourself. A number of times equal to your {{Wisdom modifier (min 1)|set from your stats on your character sheet}}, which you regain when you finish a long rest, when you are hit by an attack while you have a String attached to another creature within 10 feet of you, you can redirect the attack's damage onto that stringed creature instead of resolving it against you: it takes the full damage of the hit and you take none. Redirecting resolves the attack, so you cannot also Parry the same hit, and it costs you no action or reaction. The wire yanks it into the path — an unwilling creature gets no save; a willing ally may choose to refuse.

features.name: Manipulate
features.description: As an action, choose any number of creatures you currently have a String attached to, each within 60 feet of you. For each one, you pull or push it up to 15 feet in a direction of your choice; this forced movement doesn't provoke opportunity attacks and can drag a creature over or around minor obstacles it could normally cross, but never through solid objects or off a fall it can't survive without consent. An unwilling creature resists: it makes a Charisma saving throw against your control DC, and on a success it isn't moved. A willing creature — typically an ally — is simply moved, no save required, letting you yank a friend out of danger or into position exactly as freely as you yank a foe into a wall. Manipulate deals no damage on its own.

features.name: Tangle
features.description: As an action, choose one creature you have a String attached to within 60 feet. If it is unwilling, it must succeed on a Charisma saving throw against your control DC or become restrained as wire cinches tight around it; at the end of each of its turns it may repeat the save, ending the restrained condition on a success. If it is willing — an ally you're steadying rather than binding — you instead brace it with your strings, granting it advantage on the next Strength or Dexterity saving throw it makes before the start of your next turn. Measured control: this holds a target in place or steadies a friend, it doesn't end a fight by itself.

features.name: Disrupting Pull
features.description: When a creature you have a String attached to makes an attack roll, you can use your reaction to wrench its wire; it must succeed on a Charisma saving throw against your control DC or have disadvantage on that attack roll. Alternatively, when a creature you have a String attached to is about to take a reaction (an opportunity attack, for instance), you can use your reaction to yank its string instead; it must succeed on a Charisma saving throw against your control DC or that reaction fizzles, accomplishing nothing. Either use spends your one reaction for the round, the same reaction Parry and opportunity attacks draw from — you get one disruption, one Parry, or one opportunity attack per round, never a combination.

features.name: Guiding Pull
features.description: When a creature you have a String attached to and that is willing to be moved (typically an ally) is targeted by an attack you can see, you can use your reaction to yank it up to 10 feet toward you or to another space within your leash range, before the attack roll is made. If this pulls the target out of the attacker's reach (for a melee attack) or breaks the attacker's line of sight or range (for a ranged attack), the attack automatically misses; otherwise it resolves normally against the target's new position. This draws on the same reaction as Parry, Disrupting Pull, and opportunity attacks — you still get only one reaction per round.

features.name: Grand Marionette
features.description: As an action, choose one hostile creature you have a String attached to within 60 feet. It must make a Charisma saving throw against your control DC. On a failure, you seize its body for this turn: you may move it up to its speed (its movement can provoke opportunity attacks as normal) and force it to make a single weapon attack, using its own statistics and weapon, against a target of your choice within its reach or range. Resolve that attack normally, with the creature rolling its own attack and damage. This is your only 'puppet an action' effect at these levels — it grants exactly one forced attack, not a full turn's worth of actions, and the controlled creature otherwise loses the rest of its action and its bonus action for the turn (it keeps its reaction). You can use Grand Marionette once per turn, and only on a creature you already have a String on.

features.name: Web of Strings
features.description: Your control reaches farther and holds more of it at once: your leash range increases from 60 to 90 feet for every String-based feature you have, and your String cap increases by 1 in addition to whatever your proficiency bonus already grants it.

keyStats.label: Control DC
keyStats.note: Save DC for your String control effects (Manipulate, Tangle, Grand Marionette, etc.).

keyStats.label: Strings Cap
keyStats.note: Most active Strings you can maintain.

keyStats.label: Parry Base DC
keyStats.note: Worst parrier in the roster; he avoids melee. Lower is better.

classes.name: Sandow
classes.flavor: The circus strongman: feats of raw strength turned into a wall nothing gets past — plant your feet, brace the blow, and throw whatever's left standing.

proficiencies.skills: Choose two from Athletics, Intimidation, Perception, Performance, and Survival

classes.parryReskin: You don't dodge a hit at all — you plant your feet, turn your shoulder or weapon into the line of the blow, and let your frame do what a lesser body couldn't. A Sandow's Parry is a brace, never a step back.
classes.riposte: On a Full Dodge (your Parry d20 roll comes up strictly above your effective Parry DC), the braced impact drives the triggering attacker back: it is pushed 5 feet directly away from you, no save required, at no cost beyond the Parry itself — a shove, not a counterattack. On that same clean brace you may also spend 1 Grit to Brace Up: gain temporary hit points equal to your {{Brace Up|proficiency bonus + Constitution modifier}}, lasting until the start of your next turn (like all temporary hit points these don't stack — a new Brace Up replaces any you already have). The shove is free; the Brace Up is the paid option.

engine.name: Grit
engine.description: A pool of raw physical fortitude and adrenaline built from being in the thick of a fight — trading blows, absorbing them, hurling anything not nailed down. The Sandow's sole resource. This baseline generation is shared by every Sandow from 1st level; your Strongman's Act (chosen at 3rd level) layers its own primary Grit stream on top of it — a defensive Act builds more from taking hits, an offensive Act more from dealing them, and Human Cannonball from impacts and forced movement.
engine.generation: Gain 1 Grit the first time each turn you hit a creature with a melee attack, AND gain 1 Grit the first time each turn you are targeted by an attack from any source — whether that attack hits or misses — two separate sources, each capped at once per turn (landing a hit and being attacked in the same turn grants 2 Grit total; further hits you land or attacks made against you that same turn grant no additional Grit from that source). This works from 1st level, before any subclass is chosen.
engine.spend: Spend Grit in 1-point increments to fuel Heave, Brace, and the other feats of strength described in your features. Unspent Grit carries over between rounds but drains to 0 if you finish a short or long rest without spending it.

features.name: Grit
features.description: You gain the Grit pool, a resource built from being in the fray — landing blows and taking them — and spent to fuel your feats of strength. See your Grit engine for exactly how it is generated, spent, and capped.

features.name: Mighty Build
features.description: You count as one size larger than your actual size for the purpose of carrying capacity, and for determining whether you can grapple, shove, or throw a creature — treat a Large creature as though it were Medium, a Huge creature as though it were Large, and so on. This extends to Heave below: you can grapple and throw creatures up to one size larger than the normal rules would otherwise allow.

features.name: Iron Grip
features.description: When you hit a creature no larger than Mighty Build allows with a melee attack, you can immediately try to grab it — no extra action or attack, it rides the same hit. The target must succeed on a Strength saving throw against your {{save DC|8 + proficiency bonus + Strength modifier}} or be caught: while held it is grappled by you (its speed becomes 0) and has disadvantage on attack rolls it makes against you — pinned against your own frame, its swings at you go wide (its attacks against other creatures are unaffected). The hold has no time limit — the creature does not slip free on its own. It stays grabbed until you cash the hold in by Crushing it (see Crushing Grip) or Heaving it, until you let go or are incapacitated, or until it escapes on its turn by using its action to make a Strength (Athletics) or Dexterity (Acrobatics) check against your save DC. When a creature escapes this way it shakes free entirely and can't be caught by your Iron Grip again until the end of its next turn — so you can't chain-grab the same target, though you're free to set your hands on a different one in the meantime. (Ending the hold yourself by Crushing or Heaving the creature does not grant it this reprieve.) You can hold only one creature in your Iron Grip at a time — catching a new target releases the last. A creature held this way counts as grappled by you, so it is a valid target for Heave. Forced movement you cause with your own features (such as a Juggernaut's Bulldozer Charge) never breaks this hold — the creature is dragged with you rather than shoved free.

features.name: Heave
features.description: You grab something heavy — a creature you have grappled, or an object you're holding like a bench, a barrel, or a cage bar — and hurl it. This is used in place of one of your attacks when you take the Attack action (so at 1st level it replaces your single attack; with Extra Attack you can Heave and still swing). Spend 1 to 3 Grit: the thrown target flies 10 feet per point of Grit spent in a direction of your choice and lands in the nearest unoccupied space. What happens on landing depends on the target:

options.name: Unwilling creature or object
options.effect: A creature you already have grappled (Large or smaller, per Mighty Build), or a heavy object. It takes [[1d6]] bludgeoning damage per point of Grit spent and is knocked prone. No save — you already won the grapple to be holding it, so the throw simply happens.

options.name: Willing ally ("fastball special")
options.effect: No damage and no prone. You place the ally in an unoccupied space within range that has a clear path from you (not through walls or closed doors), ready to act — a launch, not an attack.

features.name: Hold the Line
features.description: When a hostile creature within your reach makes a melee attack against a creature other than you, you can use your reaction to make one melee weapon attack against the attacker. Additionally, whenever a hostile creature within your reach moves in a way that provokes an opportunity attack from you and you hit it with that opportunity attack, its speed becomes 0 for the rest of the current turn — it's stopped where it stands instead of completing its move. Hold the Line draws on the same single reaction as Parry and any other reaction-costed feature you have: punish an attacker or a fleeing creature, or hold your reaction in reserve to Parry — never both on the same round. This is not mind control: a creature is never forced to act against its will, only stopped or made to pay for what it already chose to do.

features.name: Brace
features.description: When you take damage from an attack — including one you failed to Parry, or one you had no reaction available to Parry — you can spend Grit, in 1-point increments and at no action cost, to reduce that damage by your {{Brace Soak|2 + your Sandow level}} per point of Grit spent, applied after any Parry multiplier and before the damage is otherwise resolved. The soak climbs with every Sandow level rather than sitting at a flat number that bigger hits quickly outpace, so a point of Grit spent on Brace stays worth it at high levels. Because Brace costs no action or reaction, it stacks with whatever reaction-costed response (or lack of one) you used for the same hit, including a round where your reaction already went to Hold the Line or a Parry. You can spend at most 2 Grit against a single instance of damage, and Brace cannot reduce damage below 0.

features.name: Crushing Grip
features.description: Your hold is a weapon in its own right. While you have a creature caught in your Iron Grip, two things become true. First, the grip turns mobile and sticky: you can drag the held creature with you when you move (it moves with you at no extra cost, staying grappled), and it has disadvantage on any check it makes to escape your grip. Second, you can crush it: in place of one attack when you take the Attack action, spend 1 Grit to deal [[1d6+Str]] bludgeoning damage to the held creature — no attack roll, the grip crushes down and the damage simply lands. You then slam the wrung-out body to the ground: the crushed creature is knocked prone, and the impact ripples outward as a shockwave — each other creature within 5 feet of it must succeed on a Strength saving throw against your save DC or be knocked prone as well. Crushing spends the hold: once you Crush a creature it is no longer grabbed, so to lock it again you must catch it fresh with a new hit. With Extra Attack you can Crush with one attack and make a normal attack with the other to re-catch it (or catch a different target), so a target barely leaves your hands. Unlike Heave, Crushing Grip moves no one — it's a stationary finisher that flattens the held creature and staggers everything pressed in around it.

meta.save: Str save vs your DC (shockwave, nearby creatures)

features.name: Extra Attack
features.description: You can attack twice, instead of once, whenever you take the Attack action on your turn.

features.name: Earthshaker
features.description: As an action, spend 3 Grit to slam a weapon, fist, or heavy object into the ground and send a shockwave through every creature of your choice within 15 feet of you. Each must succeed on a Strength saving throw against your {{save DC|8 + proficiency bonus + Strength modifier}} or be pushed 5 feet directly away from you and knocked prone. Earthshaker deals no damage on its own — it's forced movement and control, a battlefield reset, not another source of hurt. You can use Earthshaker once per turn.

keyStats.label: Save DC
keyStats.note: Save DC for effects your features force (e.g. Heave, Earthshaker).

keyStats.label: Grit Cap
keyStats.note: Most Grit you can hold at once.

keyStats.label: Brace Soak
keyStats.formula: 2 + your Sandow level, per point of Grit spent (max 2 Grit)
keyStats.note: Damage Brace removes, after any Parry multiplier.

keyStats.label: Parry Base DC
keyStats.note: Second-best parrier; lower is better.

subclasses.name: Anarchist
subclasses.flavor: The demolition act of the deck: a Joker who trades sleight of hand for lit fuses, packing his cards with flash powder and treating the whole crowd, friend or foe, as fair game for the blast radius.

features.name: Powder Burst
features.description: As an action, spend 2 Mayhem to hurl a spray of flash-powder cards that detonate in a 10-foot-radius sphere centered on a point you can see within 60 feet. Each creature in that area must make a Dexterity saving throw against your gambit DC, taking [[2d6]] fire damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one. You can use Powder Burst only once per turn, and only when you have Mayhem to spend on it — it draws on the same small pool as everything else you do, so it is bounded by how much chaos you've actually generated this fight, not something you can chain turn after turn.

features.name: Collateral Chaos
features.description: Your chaos never stays contained to one target. The first time each turn you resolve a Wild Card result or a Gambit option that targets a single creature, and a second valid target for that same effect (an additional hostile creature if the original target was hostile, or an additional willing ally if the original was an ally) is within 5 feet of the original target, you may extend the effect to that second creature as well. The second creature is affected exactly as the first, including making its own saving throw if the effect calls for one. This costs no additional Mayhem, happens as part of the Wild Card or Gambit you're already resolving (not a separate action or reaction), and can happen only once per turn.

features.name: Demolitionist's Eye
features.description: You read a building the way a card sharp reads a table: where it's weak, where it's load-bearing, and exactly what a charge in the wrong pocket would do to it. By studying a structure, wall, door, or large object for 1 minute, you learn its weak points, what it would take to bring it down, and how it would fall. You have advantage on ability checks made to break, breach, or dismantle objects and structures, and your damage against objects and structures is doubled. You can also pop a harmless flash or bang from a treated card at any point you can see within 60 feet — a distraction, a signal, or a very convincing bluff.
features.sheetSummary: Study a structure/object 1 min: learn its weak points. Advantage on checks to break/breach objects; double damage vs objects and structures. Pop a harmless flash-bang at a point within 60 ft.

subclasses.name: Bulwark
subclasses.flavor: The tank's discipline: a Sandow who plants himself between the crowd and the danger and simply refuses to move, soaking up every blow the show throws at him and daring anything to get past.

features.name: Iron Resolve
features.description: Your body is trained to feed off danger, your own and the danger aimed at those you're shielding. In addition to your baseline Grit generation, you gain 1 additional Grit the first time each turn you are targeted by an attack from any source (whether it hits or misses), and you gain 1 Grit the first time each turn a hostile creature targets an ally within 5 feet of you with an attack (attacks against you are already covered by the first clause) — two separate sources, each capped at once per turn, exactly like your baseline Grit generation. Standing at the front and drawing fire, onto himself or off an ally, is how a Bulwark keeps his Grit topped up.

features.name: Guardian's Wall
features.description: You make yourself the obstacle nothing gets past. Its protection works three ways:

options.name: Challenge (taunt)
options.effect: While you have at least 1 Grit, every enemy within 10 feet of you has disadvantage on any attack roll that does not include you as a target — swing at your allies and your bulk is always in the line. An enemy can still act freely by targeting you instead, or by stepping out of your 10-foot reach.

options.name: Intercept
options.effect: Once per turn, when a creature you can see within 5 feet of you is hit by an attack, you can throw yourself into the blow — the attack's damage is dealt to you instead of the original target. This costs you nothing and does not use your reaction, so it never competes with Parry or Hold the Line. You may reduce the damage with Brace as normal, but you cannot Parry a hit you Intercept — it has already landed.

options.name: Braced Footing
options.effect: While you have at least 1 Grit, you have advantage on saving throws to avoid being knocked prone or forcibly moved.

features.name: Living Barricade
features.description: You are a wall that walks, and everyone who has ever tried to get past you knows it. Outside combat, you count as one size larger and have advantage on any check made to block a passage, hold a door or gate shut, anchor a rope or a person, or resist being moved or dragged. A doorway, corridor, or gap you choose to fill with your body cannot be squeezed past without your permission.
features.sheetSummary: Count as one size larger + advantage on checks to block passages, hold doors shut, anchor ropes/people, or resist being moved. A gap you fill can't be squeezed past without your consent.

subclasses.name: Contortionist
subclasses.flavor: The escape artist's discipline: a body trained to fold, slip, and pour itself through gaps that were never meant to admit a person, and to shrug off every rope, cage, or grip a captor thinks is secure.

features.name: Contortion
features.description: You gain a pool of 4 Contortion Dice (d6), which you regain when you finish a long rest. When a creature you can see hits you with an attack, before you decide whether to Parry, you can spend one Contortion Die — this costs no reaction and no action — and roll it. On a 4 or higher you fold your body around the strike: you take no damage from the attack, the attacker takes half of the damage it would have dealt you (rounded down, of the same type), and your reaction stays free. On a 3 or lower the fold fails and the die is spent — the hit is not turned aside, but you may still spend your reaction to Parry it as normal if you have a reaction available; if your reaction is already gone, the attack lands in full. Because Contortion is not itself a Parry, a successful fold does not trigger Counterflow, Flowing Reflexes, or Riposte, and it sits in front of your Parry as an extra, die-gated chance to avoid a hit.

features.name: Reversal
features.description: A successful contortion doesn't just save you — it wrong-foots your attacker. Immediately after your Contortion succeeds (you roll 4 or higher on a Contortion Die and fold a hit away), you may move up to 10 feet without provoking opportunity attacks, and the next attack roll you make before the end of your next turn has advantage. The attacker overcommitted on a blow that found only empty air, and you take the opening.

features.name: Boneless Counter
features.description: When a creature within 5 feet of you hits you with an attack — the moment your Contortion fold and your Parry have both failed and the blow has actually landed — you can use your reaction to pour your body along the line of the strike and snap back from an angle it can't cover. Make one melee weapon attack against that creature; on a hit, you immediately flow up to 10 feet without provoking opportunity attacks, leaving it striking at the space you just left. This draws on the same single reaction as Parry, so on most rounds it is the counter you take when your defense has already failed you. On a turn when Flowing Reflexes (4th level) hands your reaction back after a clean Parry, it becomes a second, offensive use of that same reaction against the next foe to land a hit. The reactive slip is off-turn movement and generates no Momentum.

features.name: Sideshow Escapist
features.description: Your act is escape itself, and no lock, rope, or cage in the show has ever held you through a full performance. You can squeeze through any gap wide enough for your head, moving at half speed while you do. Given 1 minute of wriggling, you automatically escape any nonmagical restraint — ropes, chains, manacles, a straitjacket, a locked stock. You also have advantage on Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) and Dexterity (Acrobatics) checks made to slip bonds discreetly or to hide in cramped spaces a person shouldn't fit.
features.sheetSummary: Squeeze through any head-sized gap at half speed. Escape any nonmagical restraint automatically in 1 minute. Advantage on checks to slip bonds discreetly or hide in cramped spaces.

subclasses.name: Firebrand
subclasses.flavor: The fire act: a juggler whose cascade is a ring of burning orbs, trading the clean kill for spreading flame and scorched ground nobody wants to cross.

features.name: Ignite
features.description: Your implements trail fire. A creature hit by at least one of your implements on your turn is set alight and takes [[1d4]] fire damage at the start of its next turn — this is added on top of the implement's normal damage, not a replacement for it, and the die scales as your implements do. The flames flare at the start of the creature's turn and then gutter out, so there is no window for it to smother them first; hit it again and it simply burns again the following turn.

features.name: Wildfire
features.description: Once on each of your turns, one implement you throw can burst on impact instead of ricocheting: each creature within 10 feet of the space it strikes must make a Dexterity saving throw against your {{Trick Shot DC|6 + proficiency bonus + Intelligence modifier}}, taking [[2d4]] fire damage on a failure or half as much on a success. That space then burns until the start of your next turn — the first time a creature enters it on a turn, or ends its turn there, it takes [[1d4]] fire damage.

features.name: Fire-Eater's Trade
features.description: Before the fire was a weapon, it was the act. You can eat, breathe, and dance with flame harmlessly for a crowd. You can light or snuff any mundane flame you can see within 30 feet — a candle, a torch, a campfire — at will, no action required on your turn. Ordinary open flames can't harm you (fire damage from attacks, spells, and hazards still can), your implements can burn as torches without being consumed, and you have advantage on Charisma (Performance) checks that use fire.
features.sheetSummary: Light/snuff any mundane flame within 30 ft at will. Ordinary open flames can't harm you (fire damage from attacks/spells/hazards still can). Implements burn as torches unconsumed. Advantage on fire Performance checks.

subclasses.name: Gambler
subclasses.flavor: The card-sharp's discipline: a Joker who trusts the deck more than his own hands, having learned exactly which rolls can be nudged in his favor, which bets are worth doubling, and which are strictly for suckers.

features.name: Stacked Deck
features.description: You've learned to load the deck in your favor. A number of times equal to your {{Charisma modifier (min 1)|set from your stats on your character sheet}}, which you regain when you finish a long rest, you can do one of the following, no action required: choose the result of a Wild Card draw instead of rolling the d8; reroll a d20 you just rolled for your own attack roll, ability check, or saving throw and use the new result; or target one creature within 30 feet of you that just made an attack roll or saving throw, forcing it to succeed on a Charisma saving throw against your gambit DC or reroll that d20 and use the new result. These uses don't cost Mayhem and are separate from your Gambit's Rewrite the Draw option — the two don't share a pool.

meta.save: Cha save vs Gambit DC (forced reroll)

features.name: Double Down
features.description: You can bet on your own luck before you know how a roll lands. Once per turn, before making an attack roll, ability check, or saving throw (not a Parry roll), you can spend 1 Mayhem to roll a d6 and add it to the total. If that d6 comes up a 1, the bet turns on you: you take [[1d4]] psychic damage as the trick backfires, in addition to whatever the roll itself produces. You must decide to Double Down before the roll's outcome is known.

features.name: Card Sharp
features.description: You've sat at every kind of table and walked away from all of them ahead. By handling the pieces, you instantly recognize loaded dice, marked cards, or any rigged game of chance. In games of chance or gambling skill against nonmagical opposition you have advantage on any check involved, and you can cheat without being caught by anything short of magic (opposed Sleight of Hand checks made to cheat are rolled with advantage). You also have advantage on Wisdom (Insight) checks to tell whether someone is bluffing about a bet, a deal, or a debt.
features.sheetSummary: Auto-detect rigged games by handling them. Advantage on game-of-chance checks and on cheating undetected (vs nonmagical opposition). Advantage on Insight vs bluffs in bets, deals, and debts.

subclasses.name: Harlequin
subclasses.flavor: The maddest act under the big top: a Joker who stops treating melee as something to avoid and starts treating a ring full of enemies as the best possible stage, grinning wider the closer the danger gets.

features.name: Reckless Grin
features.description: As a bonus action, spend 1 Mayhem to throw caution to the wind until the start of your next turn: you have advantage on melee attack rolls you make with a finesse weapon you're proficient with, but attack rolls against you also have advantage until the start of your next turn. You can only be under the effect of Reckless Grin once at a time; using it again while it is already active changes nothing and doesn't cost additional Mayhem.

features.name: Center of the Ring
features.description: You thrive surrounded by danger. At the start of your turn, if you are within 5 feet of at least two hostile creatures, you gain temporary hit points equal to your {{Charisma modifier (min 1)|set from your stats on your character sheet}}, and for the rest of the turn moving through a space occupied by a hostile creature costs you no extra movement (you still can't end your movement there).
