As with temporary hit points from any other source, these don't stack — if you already have some, keep whichever total is higher.

features.name: Jester's License
features.description: The fool may say what the courtier cannot. When you frame it as performance — a jest, a song, a pantomime — you can deliver insults, accusations, and uncomfortable truths without your audience turning violent: creatures may eject you, despise you, or plot quietly, but they don't move to harm you for the words alone. You also have advantage on Charisma (Performance) checks, and on Charisma (Persuasion) checks made while playing the fool.
features.sheetSummary: Insults/accusations/hard truths delivered as performance don't provoke violence (targets may still eject or resent you). Advantage on Performance checks and on Persuasion while playing the fool.

subclasses.name: Human Cannonball
subclasses.flavor: The mobility discipline: a Sandow who treats his own body as the act's headline stunt, hurling himself across the ring as a living projectile and slamming into whatever, or whoever, is waiting on the other side.

features.name: Kinetic Buildup
features.description: You build charge the way a cannon builds pressure, through speed and impact. In addition to your baseline Grit generation, you gain 1 additional Grit the first time each turn you move at least half your speed in a straight line and then hit a creature with a melee attack before the end of that turn, and you gain 1 Grit the first time each turn a creature takes damage from your Cannon Launch — the impact of a landing slam feeding your next charge. Two separate sources, each capped at once per turn, exactly like your baseline Grit generation.

features.name: Cannon Launch
features.description: As a bonus action, spend 2 Grit to launch yourself up to 30 feet in a straight line to an unoccupied space you can see. This movement doesn't provoke opportunity attacks and never causes fall damage from the arc, however dramatic it looks. If you end that movement within 5 feet of a hostile creature, or pass through a hostile creature's space en route (stopping in the nearest available space when you do), that creature must succeed on a Strength saving throw against your {{save DC|8 + proficiency bonus + Strength modifier}} or take [[2d6]] bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. You can use Cannon Launch only once per turn. Additionally, when you use Heave's fastball special to throw a willing ally, the throw's range increases to 20 feet per point of Grit spent, instead of the normal 10.

features.name: Death-Defying Stunt
features.description: Launching yourself at things is not just a combat trick — it's the act. Your long jump distance is doubled and your high jump increases by 10 feet. You land any deliberate jump or drop of 30 feet or less on your feet, unharmed and looking spectacular. You also have advantage on Charisma (Performance) and Dexterity (Acrobatics) checks made as part of a stunt.
features.sheetSummary: Long jump doubled; high jump +10 ft. Land any deliberate jump/drop of 30 ft or less unharmed. Advantage on Performance and Acrobatics checks made as part of a stunt.

subclasses.name: Impalement
subclasses.flavor: The impalement act: a juggler who doesn't spray steel but places it — one studied target, every blade finding the same fatal seam.

features.name: Mark
features.description: As a bonus action, you can Mark one creature you can see — a cold read of its guard and rhythm. Until the end of your next turn, your thrown implements score a critical hit against the Marked creature on a roll of 19 or 20, and the first of your implements to hit it each turn deals extra damage equal to your {{Intelligence modifier|set from your stats on your character sheet}}. You can have only one creature Marked at a time; Marking a new one ends the old.

features.name: Impossible Shot
features.description: Your calculations beat the battlefield. Your thrown implements ignore half and three-quarters cover, and they suffer no disadvantage anywhere within their full range: a normal throw reaches 20 feet, and you simply ignore the long-range penalty that would otherwise apply on throws from 20 out to 60 feet. Every angle is a clean angle.

features.name: Sideshow Precision
features.description: You have split apples on volunteers' heads for years, and you have never once hit the volunteer. Your thrown implements automatically hit any stationary object target you can see within range — no roll needed. You can pin a willing or unaware creature's clothing to a nearby surface with a throw, holding it in place until it spends an action tearing free. You never hit an unintended creature with a thrown implement, and you have advantage on Charisma (Performance) checks built around throwing.
features.sheetSummary: Thrown implements auto-hit stationary objects in range. Pin a willing/unaware creature's clothing to a surface (it spends an action to tear free). Never hit bystanders by accident. Advantage on throwing-act Performance checks.

subclasses.name: Impersonator
subclasses.flavor: The discipline of the mimic: a Doppelganger who spends less time studying combat forms and more time studying people — their faces, their voices, their little tells — until borrowing a life, or a single trick from one, is second nature.

features.name: Perfect Mimicry
features.description: You have practiced copying more than a silhouette. As an action, you can assume the exact physical appearance — clothing, minor scars, voice, and posture included — of any Medium or Small humanoid you have observed for at least 1 minute, for up to 1 hour or until you end it early (no action required) or use this feature again. This changes only your outward appearance, not your game statistics, and any equipment you're wearing or carrying isn't transformed along with you. While disguised this way, you have advantage on Charisma (Deception) checks made to pass yourself off as that specific individual, and a creature without a particular reason to be suspicious can't tell you're a fake without succeeding on a Wisdom (Insight) check contested by your Deception check (or other means, such as true seeing). You can use this feature a number of times equal to your {{proficiency bonus|set from your stats on your character sheet}}, regaining all expended uses when you finish a long rest.
features.sheetSummary: Action: assume the exact appearance/voice of a Medium or Small humanoid observed for 1+ min (1 h, appearance only, gear unchanged). Advantage on Deception to pass as them; seeing through it takes an Insight check vs your Deception. Prof bonus uses / long rest.

features.name: Borrowed Trick
features.description: You study how others fight and echo the shape of their strikes through your reflections. When a creature you can see attacks you or an ally within 30 feet of you — whether the attack hits or misses — you can memorize the damage type of that attack. You hold one memorized type at a time; memorizing a new one replaces it, and it fades when the fight ends. As a bonus action, you can destroy one of your Clones within 5 feet of a target: make your normal attack roll, and on a hit the Clone deals [[1d6+Con]] damage of the memorized type. The Clone copies only the damage type — never the original attack's damage dice, riders, or special effects. (If a paladin smites you, you don't gain the smite; your Clone simply deals this [[1d6]] as radiant.) You can do this a number of times equal to your {{proficiency bonus|set from your stats on your character sheet}}, regaining all uses on a long rest.
features.sheetSummary: When a creature attacks you or an ally within 30 ft (hit or miss), memorize its damage type (hold one, lasts the fight). Bonus action, destroy a Clone within 5 ft of a target: normal attack roll, on hit [[1d6]] + Con of the memorized type. Copies the type only, not the attack's dice/riders/effects. Prof bonus uses / long rest.

features.name: Treacherous Opening
features.description: A borrowed face is a weapon the moment you stop needing it. Once per combat, the first attack roll you make against a creature that currently believes a Perfect Mimicry disguise of yours is genuine is made with advantage and deals an extra [[1d6]] damage on a hit. Making an attack immediately ends the deception for every creature that sees it — the face stays, but nobody believes it anymore.
features.sheetSummary: 1/combat: first attack vs a creature fooled by your active Perfect Mimicry has advantage + extra [[1d6]] damage on hit. Attacking ends the deception for all who see it.

subclasses.name: Juggernaut
subclasses.flavor: The offense discipline: a Sandow who never stops moving forward, turning every hit, shove, and thrown body into fuel for the next, bigger swing.

features.name: Unstoppable Force
features.description: You feed on the wreckage you cause. In addition to your baseline Grit generation, you gain 1 additional Grit the first time each turn you deal damage to a creature with a Heave throw, and you gain 1 Grit the first time each turn you knock a creature prone by some means OTHER than a Heave (a shove, Bulldozer Charge, Earthshaker, and the like) — two separate sources, each capped at once per turn, exactly like your baseline Grit generation. A Heave already pays out through the first source, so the prone it also causes never grants a second Grit off the same throw. The bigger the mess you make, the more you have left to make it worse.

features.name: Bulldozer Charge
features.description: When you move at least 15 feet in a straight line toward a creature and then hit it with a melee weapon attack before the end of the same turn, the attack deals an extra [[1d6]] bludgeoning damage, and the target must succeed on a Strength saving throw against your {{save DC|8 + proficiency bonus + Strength modifier}} or be knocked prone and pushed 5 feet directly away from you, your choice of direction. You can use Bulldozer Charge only once per turn.

features.name: Through, Not Around
features.description: Doors are a suggestion and walls are a delay. You have advantage on Strength checks made to break, force, or topple objects and barriers, and your attacks deal double damage to objects and structures. Given a minute of uninterrupted battering, you can break through any nonmagical door, palisade, or barricade that a creature of your size could conceivably breach — the only question the table needs to answer is how much noise you make doing it.
features.sheetSummary: Advantage on Strength checks to break/force objects and barriers; double damage vs objects and structures. Given 1 minute, breach any breachable nonmagical door or barricade.

subclasses.name: Mirror
subclasses.flavor: The discipline of the counter-strike: a Doppelganger who turns every shattered reflection and every last-second sidestep into consequence for the creature that struck first.

features.name: Shattering Reflection
features.description: Your Clones don't break quietly. Whenever a creature destroys one of your Clones with an attack roll (including a Clone destroyed by an attack redirected onto it via Mirror Image, but never a Clone you destroy yourself through one of your own features), that creature must make a Dexterity saving throw against your {{save DC|8 + proficiency bonus + Constitution modifier}}. On a failed save, it takes [[1d6+Con]] psychic damage as shards of the shattered illusion rake across it, and it has disadvantage on the next attack roll it makes before the end of its next turn. On a successful save, it takes half as much damage and suffers no other effect. This triggers at most once per turn (no matter how many Clones are destroyed that turn) and requires no action or reaction from you to resolve.

features.name: Vanishing Trick
features.description: Your Swap doubles as a parting insult. Once per turn, when you use Swap, you may choose one creature within 5 feet of the space you just left that could see you there: that creature has disadvantage on the next attack roll it makes before the end of your next turn, having lunged for a reflection that was never really there. This requires no action or reaction beyond using Swap itself, and doesn't change how Swap otherwise works. In addition, once per combat you may Swap defensively: when a creature you can see hits you with an attack, before its damage is dealt, you can use your reaction to Swap with one of your Clones within Swap's range — you trade places, the blow lands on the Clone now standing in the space you left (destroying it), and you take no damage. This defensive use costs your reaction (unlike the disadvantage effect above) and needs a Clone available to Swap with.

features.name: Flawless Forgery
features.description: A reflection doesn't have to stay in the glass. As an action, you can create a mirror-copy of a Small or smaller nonmagical object you have held within the last minute. The copy is identical to close inspection but is all surface: it shatters into harmless light if it takes any damage, and dissolves on its own after 1 hour. A forged key opens nothing and a forged coin won't survive a bite test — but for a swap, a con, or a decoy on a cushion, it is perfect. You can use this feature a number of times equal to your {{proficiency bonus|set from your stats on your character sheet}}, regaining all expended uses when you finish a long rest.
features.sheetSummary: Action: create a mirror-copy of a Small or smaller nonmagical object held in the last minute — perfect to inspection, shatters on any damage, lasts 1 h. Prof bonus uses / long rest.

subclasses.name: Puppet Master
subclasses.flavor: The offensive discipline of the wire: a Puppeteer who stops treating strung enemies as mere targets and starts treating them as weapons, turning a foe's own momentum, and its own allies, against it.

features.name: Turncoat Strings
features.description: When a hostile creature you have a String attached to hits a target with an attack roll, you can use your reaction to wrench its wire mid-swing, forcing it to make a Charisma saving throw against your control DC. On a failure, you redirect the attack toward a different creature of your choice: a creature within the attacker's reach (for a melee attack) or within its range and your line of sight (for a ranged attack). Compare the triggering attack roll to the new target's AC — if it hits, the attack strikes the new target instead (roll its damage against them); if it would miss the new target, the attack misses entirely. On a success, the attack resolves against its original target as normal. You can use Turncoat Strings only once per turn, and it draws on the same reaction as Parry, Disrupting Pull, Guiding Pull, and opportunity attacks — you still get only one reaction per round. This is a single, save-gated redirection of one attack, not a seizure of the creature's turn: it grants you no movement or additional actions from the target the way Grand Marionette does.

features.name: Puppet's Spite
features.description: Your Control Wires cut deeper into a foe already caught in your web. When you hit a creature you have a String attached to with a Control Wires attack, it takes extra damage equal to your {{Wisdom modifier (min 1)|set from your stats on your character sheet}}. You can deal this extra damage only once per turn. This extra damage applies only to unwilling creatures — it never triggers against a willing creature, such as an ally you've strung for support rather than control.

features.name: Deft Wires
features.description: Your wires are fine enough to vanish in lamplight and precise enough to sign your name. As an action, you can manipulate an object within 30 feet of you as though with your own hands: open a latch, pull a lever, lift or carry up to 10 pounds, pour a drink, palm a key from a belt. Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) checks made this way can target creatures and objects at that range, and you have advantage on them when your hands are visibly elsewhere — nobody suspects the man across the room.
features.sheetSummary: Action: manipulate an object within 30 ft as if by hand (open, pull, lift up to 10 lb, pickpocket at range). Advantage on Sleight of Hand made this way while your hands are visibly elsewhere.

subclasses.name: Ringmaster
subclasses.flavor: The showman: a juggler who works the whole ring, turning heavy clubs and sheer spectacle into crowd control — every eye on him, whether they like it or not.

features.name: All Eyes On Me
features.description: You command attention like a spotlight. Once on each of your turns, when you hit a creature with an implement, you can forgo that implement's damage to instead rivet its attention on you: it must succeed on a Wisdom saving throw against your {{Trick Shot DC|6 + proficiency bonus + Intelligence modifier}} or have disadvantage on any attack roll that doesn't include you as a target, until the start of your next turn. You can hold only one creature's attention this way at a time.

features.name: Grand Finale
features.description: Once per short or long rest, you can turn a Barrage into a showstopping set piece. Take the Attack action and throw implements as normal, then put on the show: each ally who can see you gains temporary hit points equal to your Juggler level, and each enemy within 15 feet of you must succeed on a Wisdom saving throw against your {{Trick Shot DC|6 + proficiency bonus + Intelligence modifier}} or be frightened of you until the end of its next turn. The crowd is yours.

features.name: Command the Crowd
features.description: The ring taught you that any room is an audience that hasn't been addressed yet. Your voice carries clearly up to 300 feet without shouting. You have advantage on Charisma checks made to address, sway, or intimidate a group of three or more creatures at once, and with a word and a gesture you can pull a nonhostile crowd's attention onto yourself — cutting through noise, panic, or a rival speaker mid-sentence.
features.sheetSummary: Voice carries 300 ft clearly. Advantage on Charisma checks against groups of 3+ creatures. Can seize a nonhostile crowd's attention at will (cuts through noise/panic).

subclasses.name: Swarm
subclasses.flavor: The discipline of overwhelming numbers: a Doppelganger who stops treating a Clone as one spare body and starts fielding a crowd, letting sheer duplication wear a target down from every angle at once.

features.name: Overflowing Reflections
features.description: Your reflections multiply more readily than most. Your Clone cap increases by 1 (this stacks with the increase Hall of Mirrors grants at 5th level). Additionally, once per combat, when you use your bonus action to create a Clone, you may create a second Clone at the same time, provided doing so would not exceed your (increased) Clone cap; both appear following the normal rules for Clone creation, in unoccupied spaces you can see within range.

features.name: Overwhelming Numbers
features.description: You press your numbers from every angle at once — and then take the legs out. Once per turn, when you hit a creature with a weapon attack while one of your Clones is within 5 feet of it, two things happen: you deal an extra [[1d4]] damage of the weapon's damage type as the creature flinches from a second angle it can't track, and the Clone sweeps in at the same instant, forcing the target to succeed on a Constitution saving throw against your {{save DC|8 + proficiency bonus + Constitution modifier}} or be knocked prone. Both happen at most once per turn no matter how many Clones are adjacent, and this combines with the advantage granted by Flanking Reflection.

meta.save: Con save vs your DC (or prone)

features.name: One-Man Crowd
features.description: Why blend into a crowd when you can bring your own? Outside combat, your Clones can wear faces that aren't yours — distinct strangers rather than copies — and can mill about within 60 feet of you: enough to fake a gathering, an entourage, a queue, or a very busy alibi. You have advantage on ability checks made to blend into, start, or read a crowd, and you know instantly when one of your Clones is destroyed, and where it was.
features.sheetSummary: Out of combat, Clones can look like distinct strangers and mill within 60 ft (fake crowds, entourages, alibis). Advantage on checks to blend into/start/read crowds. Know instantly when and where a Clone is destroyed.

subclasses.name: Tangleweaver
subclasses.flavor: The battlefield-control discipline of the wire: a Puppeteer who stops stringing up creatures one at a time and starts filling whole patches of ground with wire, so that walking into the show at all becomes the mistake.

features.name: Snare Weave
features.description: As an action, you weave loose wire across the ground in a 15-foot square within 60 feet of you, filling it with a low, near-invisible lattice that lasts for 1 minute or until you dismiss it (no action required). The area is difficult terrain to any creature without a String attached to you. Any creature other than you that enters the area for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there must succeed on a Charisma saving throw against your control DC or become restrained by the wire. A creature restrained this way can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the restrained condition on itself on a success, or use its action to make a Strength or Dexterity check (its choice) against your control DC to tear free instead. You can use Snare Weave a number of times equal to your {{Wisdom modifier (min 1)|set from your stats on your character sheet}}, regaining all expended uses when you finish a long rest.

features.name: Binding Knots
features.description: Your bindings compound on themselves. When you use Tangle, you can target one additional creature you have a String attached to within range, provided the two targets are within 15 feet of each other and you don't mix a willing target with an unwilling one in the same use. You can use this double-target option a number of times equal to your {{Wisdom modifier (min 1)|set from your stats on your character sheet}}, regaining all expended uses when you finish a long rest.

features.name: Wired Room
features.description: You never sleep anywhere you haven't strung first. Over 10 minutes, you can rig an area up to a 30-foot square — a room, a campsite, a corridor — with a lattice of near-invisible trip-lines that lasts 8 hours or until you dismantle it. You know instantly when a Small or larger creature enters the area (and roughly where). When you rig the area, you can include one snare: the first creature to trigger it must succeed on a Dexterity saving throw against your Control DC or be caught, restrained until it or an ally uses an action to cut it free.
features.sheetSummary: 10 min: wire up to a 30-ft square for 8 h — know instantly when a Small or larger creature enters. Optional snare: first trigger, Dex save vs Control DC or restrained until cut free (action).

subclasses.name: Tightrope
subclasses.flavor: The precision discipline: a performer who has spent so long balanced on a single strand of wire high above the crowd that solid ground feels generous, and every strike lands exactly where it was always going to.

features.name: Perfect Balance
features.description: You ignore the effects of narrow, unstable, or slippery surfaces: you can move across a tightrope, ledge, ice, rubble, or similar footing at your full speed, without needing an ability check, and you don't fall from such a surface. Additionally, you have advantage on saving throws and ability checks made to avoid being knocked prone or moved against your will.

features.name: Improved Critical
features.description: Your weapon attacks score a critical hit on a roll of 19 or 20. Whenever you score a critical hit, you regain 1 Momentum (no more than once per turn, and never above your current cap).

features.name: Wire-Walker's Riposte
features.description: You have spent years making attackers commit to a strike that finds only wire and empty air. When a creature within 5 feet of you misses you with an attack roll, you can use your reaction to answer from perfect balance: make one melee weapon attack against it. Like all your weapon attacks (see Improved Critical), this scores a critical hit on a 19 or 20. Because your own footing is never in question, on a hit you throw the target off its line — it has disadvantage on the next attack roll it makes before the start of your next turn. This is distinct from your base Riposte, which triggers on a clean Full Dodge and spends Momentum at no reaction cost; Wire-Walker's Riposte instead punishes any miss and costs your reaction. It uses the same single reaction as Parry, so landing a Parry and a Riposte in the same round relies on Flowing Reflexes (4th level) returning your reaction after a successful Parry.

features.name: Wire-Walker's Poise
features.description: Years above the crowd have calibrated your eye and your nerve. You can judge any distance, height, drop, or gap you can see to within a foot, just by looking. You have advantage on Charisma (Performance) checks that showcase balance or precision, and you can carry one willing Medium or smaller creature across a narrow or unstable surface without slowing or risking either of you.
features.sheetSummary: Judge any visible distance/height/drop exactly by eye. Advantage on balance/precision Performance checks. Carry one willing Medium or smaller creature across narrow surfaces safely at full speed.

subclasses.name: Trapeze
subclasses.flavor: The purest expression of the Acrobat's aerial fantasy: a performer who treats open air as solid ground, closing distance, escaping danger, and pulling allies along for the ride on lines only they can see.

features.name: Aerial Anchor
features.description: Your mastery of the trapeze frees you from needing real rigging. You can use Trapeze Swing at any time, even when no rope, chandelier, banner, or similar structure is within reach, treating open air as your line. Additionally, when you use Trapeze Swing, you may bring one willing or incapacitated Medium or smaller creature within 5 feet of you along the swing; that creature moves with you at no cost to your own movement and does not provoke opportunity attacks during the swing. Trapeze Swing remains a signature move when used this way, so it still generates Momentum.

features.name: Featherweight Landing
features.description: Your body has learned to always find purchase. You take no damage from a fall of 30 feet or less, and for any longer fall you calculate damage as though you had fallen 30 feet less than you actually did. In addition, whenever you fall or would be knocked prone as a result of your own fall or your own signature move (such as a failed Wall-Run or Trapeze Swing), you can spend 1 Momentum (no action required) to land on your feet instead, ending the fall without becoming prone, provided you end it in a space you could normally occupy. This does not apply to being knocked prone or forcibly moved by a hostile creature's attack, spell, or ability.

features.name: Intercepting Swing
features.description: Your lines reach anywhere you can see. When a creature you can see within 30 feet of you hits an ally with an attack, you can use your reaction to drop onto a line out of nowhere and swing to an unoccupied space within 5 feet of that attacker, then make one melee weapon attack against it — punishing the opening the instant it commits. As with Trapeze Swing, this aerial style is flashy, not safe: leaving a creature's reach as you swing provokes opportunity attacks as normal. This does not count as your Trapeze Swing for the turn and generates no Momentum. It uses the same single reaction as Parry, so on a turn when Flowing Reflexes (4th level) returns your reaction after a clean Parry, you can both defend yourself and swing to a struck ally's aid in the same round.

features.name: Show Rigging
features.description: The lines you conjure don't have to be for you alone. As an action outside combat, you can conjure up to 60 feet of shimmering rigging anchored to any two points you can see — a climbable rope, a rope ladder, or a taut line. It holds up to 500 pounds, lasts 10 minutes or until you dismiss it (no action required), and you can maintain only one at a time. You also have advantage on ability checks involving ropes, knots, and rigging.
features.sheetSummary: Action (out of combat): conjure up to 60 ft of climbable rigging between two visible anchor points (holds 500 lb, lasts 10 min, one at a time). Advantage on rope/knot/rigging checks.

subclasses.name: Warden
subclasses.flavor: The protective discipline of the wire: a Puppeteer who strings up allies not to move them like pieces on a board, but to hold a line back to them, ready to snap taut the instant one is struck.

features.name: Warding Pull
features.description: When a creature you have a String attached to is hit by an attack, you can use your reaction to snap its wire taut, guiding it half a step aside at the last instant and granting it resistance to the damage from that attack. This draws on the same reaction as Parry, Disrupting Pull, and Guiding Pull — you still get only one reaction per round, so protecting an ally this way costs the same reaction you'd otherwise spend defending yourself or disrupting a foe.

features.name: Steadfast Line
features.description: A willing creature you have a String attached to has advantage on saving throws made to avoid being knocked prone or forcibly moved, as long as it remains within your leash range — your wire keeps it steady on its feet. Additionally, if such a creature is knocked prone anyway, it can spend only 5 feet of movement (instead of half its speed) to stand back up, guided upright by the string.

features.name: Keeper's Thread
features.description: Some strings aren't for pulling — they're for holding on. Over 1 minute, you can tie a hair-fine thread to a willing creature you touch. For the next 24 hours, while the creature is within 1 mile of you, you know its direction and its general condition (unharmed, wounded, unconscious, or dead), and if it is in danger while you sleep, the thread's tug wakes you instantly. You can maintain a number of threads equal to your {{Charisma modifier (min 1)|set from your stats on your character sheet}}.
features.sheetSummary: 1-min ritual, touch, willing creature: for 24 h know its direction + condition (unharmed/wounded/unconscious/dead) within 1 mile; its danger wakes you from sleep. Cha mod threads at once (min 1).

spells.name: Fireball
components: M (a tiny ball of bat guano and sulfur)
spells.description: A bright streak flashes from your pointing finger to a point you choose within range and then blossoms with a low roar into an explosion of flame. Each creature in a 20-foot-radius sphere centered on that point must make a Dexterity saving throw. A target takes 8d6 fire damage on a failed save, or half as much on a successful one.
spells.higherLevels: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 4th level or higher, the damage increases by 1d6 for each slot level above 3rd.

skills.name: Athletics
skills.description: Covers difficult situations while climbing, jumping, or swimming, and physical feats like grappling.

skills.name: Acrobatics
skills.description: Staying on your feet in tricky situations, balancing, and performing acrobatic stunts.

skills.name: Sleight of Hand
skills.description: Perform manual trickery like planting an item, palming an object, or picking a pocket or lock without being noticed.

skills.name: Stealth
skills.description: Conceal yourself from enemies, slip past guards, or move silently without being seen or heard.

skills.name: Arcana
skills.description: Recall lore about spells, magic items, eldritch symbols, and the planes of existence.

skills.name: History
skills.description: Recall lore about historical events, legendary people, ancient kingdoms, and past disputes.

skills.name: Investigation
skills.description: Deduce clues, spot hidden details, and draw conclusions from evidence at a scene.

skills.name: Nature
skills.description: Recall lore about terrain, plants, animals, weather, and natural cycles.

skills.name: Religion
skills.description: Recall lore about deities, rites, prayers, religious hierarchies, and holy symbols.

skills.name: Animal Handling
skills.description: Calm a domesticated animal, keep a mount from bolting, or intuit an animal's intentions.

skills.name: Insight
skills.description: Determine the true intentions of a creature, such as spotting a lie or predicting its next move.

skills.name: Medicine
skills.description: Stabilize a dying companion, diagnose an illness, or identify what kind of ailment afflicts someone.

skills.name: Perception
skills.description: Spot, hear, or otherwise detect the presence of something using your senses.

skills.name: Survival
skills.description: Follow tracks, hunt, navigate the wilderness, identify natural hazards, and predict the weather.

skills.name: Deception
skills.description: Convincingly hide the truth, either verbally or through actions, such as bluffing or disguising intent.

skills.name: Intimidation
skills.description: Influence someone through overt threats, hostile actions, or physical violence.

skills.name: Performance
skills.description: Delight an audience with music, dance, acting, storytelling, or other forms of entertainment.

skills.name: Persuasion
skills.description: Influence someone with tact, social grace, or good nature.

passives.name: Great Weapon Master
passives.description: You've learned to put the weight of a weapon to your advantage. On your turn, when you score a critical hit with a melee weapon or reduce a creature to 0 hit points with one, you can make one melee weapon attack as a bonus action. Before you make a melee attack with a heavy weapon that you are proficient with, you can choose to take a -5 penalty to the attack roll. If the attack hits, you add +10 to the attack's damage.

weapons.name: Aerial Ribbons
weapons.flavor: Weighted silk streamers the Acrobat conjures and whirls — equal parts spectacle, weapon, and safety line.

parryProfile.note: Flowing silk wraps and redirects an incoming blow beautifully — one of the best defensive weapons in the roster, matching the Acrobat's dodge-first identity. Its low damage (1d4, the same reach-tax the whip pays) is the deliberate tradeoff: the ribbons are a deflection and a safety line, not a killing weapon. Signature to the Acrobat.

weapons.name: Cannonball
weapons.flavor: A solid iron sphere from the human-cannonball act, hurled by hand — it lands like a falling anvil.

parryProfile.note: A cannonball is for throwing, not fending — there is nothing to parry with, and once hurled it is gone. Falls back to the brace (unarmed) case if held.

weapons.name: Control Wires
weapons.flavor: Near-invisible monofilament strung from the fingertips — it cuts, it binds, and it answers the puppeteer's will as readily as it answers his hand. In close it threatens with a duelist's reach; at distance the wires snap taut across the room to open a wound where no blade could reach. Special: you may attack with it as a melee weapon (10 ft reach) OR as a ranged weapon (range 30/60 ft), your choice each attack — a hit lets the Strings engine attach a free String at that range.

parryProfile.note: The one weapon that can parry ranged attacks as well as melee — a curtain of wire snaps up to turn aside blade or bolt alike. The Puppeteer's only real defense, though his very high Parry DC means even this rarely lands a Full Dodge. Signature to the Puppeteer.

weapons.name: Dagger
weapons.flavor: A plain, quick blade — the universal backup, easy to hide and easy to throw.

parryProfile.note: A small blade parries only passably — better than bare hands, worse than a dueling weapon. Once thrown it is gone, with nothing left in hand to parry with.

weapons.name: Hand Crossbow
weapons.flavor: A compact one-handed crossbow, easily hidden up a sleeve until the trick needs a bolt.

parryProfile.note: A drawn, loaded crossbow is committed to the shot — there is nothing in that hand to intercept a melee blow with. Trying falls back to the empty-hands (unarmed) case.

weapons.name: Harlequin's Cane
weapons.flavor: A slim jester's cane, twirled and cracked with theatrical flourish — half prop, half bludgeon, wholly unpredictable.

parryProfile.note: A twirling cane is a natural fending tool, sweeping blows aside with a showman's flourish.

weapons.name: Iron Chain
weapons.flavor: A heavy length of tent chain swung in wide arcs — it reaches, it wraps, and it hurts.

parryProfile.note: A whirling chain can catch and foul an incoming weapon at reach, so it fends a touch better than a rigid bludgeon.

weapons.name: Juggling Clubs
weapons.flavor: Weighted performance clubs spun in a blur and hurled end over end — a juggler's bread and butter, as happy to crack a skull as to catch the light.

parryProfile.note: A club still in hand parries modestly, batting a blow aside with its weighted head. Once thrown it has left your hand — nothing to guard with. How many clubs a Juggler still holds is governed by the Juggler's engine (Deflect).

weapons.name: Mirror Blades
weapons.flavor: A matched pair of thin, silvered short blades — when the Doppelganger strikes, every one of its reflections seems to strike too.

parryProfile.note: Paired blades fend competently, and it's never quite clear which hand — or which of you — is holding them. Signature to the Doppelganger.

weapons.name: Rapier
weapons.flavor: A duelist's blade, thin and quick — as much a defensive tool as an offensive one in the right hands.

parryProfile.note: A rapier's guard and reach make it well-suited to parrying — slightly easier than a bare-fisted defense.

weapons.name: Razor Cards
weapons.flavor: A deck of steel-edged playing cards, flicked from the fingertips faster than the eye can follow — always a winning hand.

parryProfile.note: A thrown card is gone the instant it leaves the hand — nothing to fend with. Falls back to the unarmed case if you try. Signature to the Joker.

weapons.name: Shortsword
weapons.flavor: A short, straight blade — light and quick, easy to pair and easier to hide.

parryProfile.note: A balanced short blade parries competently — neutral, neither a dedicated dueling guard like a rapier nor an improvised tool.

weapons.name: Strongman's Barbell
weapons.flavor: The classic loaded bar of the strongman act, swung as a two-handed bludgeon — absurd in anyone else's hands, natural in his.

parryProfile.note: A heavy bar is clumsy to fend with, but The Sandow simply braces it across an incoming blow — neutral, leaning on his class's brace rather than the weapon's finesse.

weapons.name: Throwing Knives
weapons.flavor: A matched set of balanced blades, drawn and released in the same motion — the baseline knife every circus performer learns to spin before they learn anything flashier.

parryProfile.note: A knife held in hand can parry modestly — better than bare fists, worse than a dueling blade. Once thrown it has left your hand, so you simply have nothing in that hand to parry with (the empty-hands case). How many knives a Juggler still holds is governed by the Juggler's engine.

weapons.name: Weighted Orbs
weapons.flavor: Palm-sized iron spheres kept endlessly aloft. A Firebrand sets them alight, but even cold they land like a dropped anvil.

parryProfile.note: An orb still in hand can knock a blow aside, though its round face is a clumsier guard than a club. Once thrown it is gone. How many orbs a Juggler still holds is governed by the Juggler's engine (Deflect).

weapons.name: Whip
weapons.flavor: A long braided lash that cracks like a gunshot and keeps enemies at arm's length.

parryProfile.note: At reach a whip can snap aside or tangle an incoming weapon — a neutral parry, with the added advantage of keeping the attacker a step away.

armor.name: Beasthide Wrap
armor.flavor: Layered hides from the menagerie's beasts, roughly cured — crude, warm, and surprisingly tough.

armor.name: Boilerplate Splint
armor.flavor: Riveted iron strips over a leather backing, salvaged from the shows machinery. Heavier and pricier than chain mail, a step short of full plate — and it takes real strength to march in.

armor.name: Colossus Plate
armor.flavor: Full articulated plate built for the strongman act's grand finale — a walking fortress that only the mightiest can even carry.

armor.name: Fortune-Tellers Robes
armor.flavor: Layered silks and star-charts — the working clothes of the tent mystics. No protection to speak of, but they need no training to wear, which is the whole point for someone whose defense is their mind, not their hide.

armor.name: Gladiators Half-Plate
armor.flavor: Show-arena plate that covers most of the body but leaves the legs free. The best medium AC there is — you just clank, so forget sneaking.

armor.name: Ironmonger's Chain Mail
armor.flavor: A heavy shirt of interlocking rings from the show's smith — dependable, weighty, and loud on a quiet night.

armor.name: Padded Motley
armor.flavor: A quilted patchwork performer's costume — soft protection under bright colors, but it rustles with every step.

armor.name: Parade Shield
armor.flavor: A round, brightly painted shield twirled in the opening march — light enough to flourish, solid enough to turn a blade.

armor.name: Serpent-Scale Mail
armor.flavor: Overlapping enameled scales that shimmer like a snake charmer's prize — protective, but they hiss and clink when you try to move unseen. Cheaper cover than a breastplate for the same base AC, but the overlapping scales rattle — disadvantage on stealth.

armor.name: Strongman's Breastplate
armor.flavor: A fitted steel chest piece worn over the bare arms of a showman — solid protection that still lets the shoulders flex for a lift. Its edge over scale mail isn't AC — it's silence: the same 14 base, but no stealth penalty.

armor.name: Studded Showleather
armor.flavor: Polished leather set with gleaming rivets — as much showpiece as protection, and the best light armor a nimble performer can wear.

armor.name: Troupers Chain Shirt
armor.flavor: A light mesh worn under the costume — quiet enough to tumble in, sturdier than leather. The middle-ground medium for a performer who still wants to move.

armor.name: Tumbler's Leathers
armor.flavor: Supple, close-fitting leather that moves with the body — the acrobat's default, quiet and unrestrictive.
